Triage Data:
    • Triage Category: 3
    • Blood Pressure: 142/88 mmHg
    • Pulse: 94/min
    • SpO2: 98%
    • Temperature: 37.9°C
    • VAS: 7 - Severe pain
    • GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Reason for Visit:
    • Severe pain under the right rib cage, nausea, and vomiting.
Medical History:
The patient has a history of arterial hypertension, treated for 5 years. Known history of cholelithiasis (gallstones) diagnosed incidentally on ultrasound 2 years ago, but was asymptomatic until recently. She reports occasional "indigestion" after fatty meals but never sought emergency care. No previous abdominal surgeries. BMI 33.
Current Illness:
The patient presents with severe, constant pain in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) that began approximately 8 hours ago, shortly after a late dinner. The pain radiates to the right scapula. She has vomited twice since the onset of pain. She reports a low-grade fever at home (37.7°C). No changes in stool or urine color. No chest pain or dyspnea.
Function:
    • Appetite: Nauseated, unable to eat
    • Weight Loss: None
    • Stool: Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency
    • Urination: Normal
Habits:
    • Non-smoker
    • Occasional social alcohol consumption
Therapy:
    • Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1
    • Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1
Allergies: Penicillin (reported skin rash/hives).
Status:
    • Musculoskeletal build: Obese, conscious, cooperative, oriented, distressed due to pain, afebrile (currently 37.9°C), well-hydrated, skin and visible mucous membranes well-perfused, tongue slightly dry, midline, no peripheral lymphadenopathy.
    • Chest: Symmetrical, respiratory movements even.
    • Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing. SpO2 98%.
    • Heart: Regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs, BP 142/88 mmHg, pulse 94/min.
    • Abdomen: Above the level of the chest (adipose tissue), soft, localized tenderness in the right upper quadrant with positive Murphy's sign and mild involuntary guarding. No rebound tenderness in other quadrants. No palpable masses. Audible peristalsis. Lumbar percussion bilaterally negative.
    • Limbs: Symmetrical, no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and equal.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus rhythm, rate 92/min, physiological electrical axis, no acute ST-T wave changes.
    • Blood tests: WBC 14.8 x10^9/L (H), RBC 4.65 x10^12/L, HGB 138 g/L, HCT 0.412 L/L, MCV 88.5 fL, Platelets 245 x10^9/L, NEU% 82.1% (H), LYM% 12.4% (L), CRP 54.2 mg/L (H).
    • Biochemistry: Urea 5.1 mmol/L, Creatinine 78 µmol/L, eGFR >90 mL/min, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, AST 42 U/L, ALT 51 U/L, GGT 68 U/L (H), ALP 115 U/L, Total Bilirubin 21.4 µmol/L (slight H), Lipase 32 U/L (normal), Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L.
    • Abdominal Ultrasound (Emergency): Gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall (5.2 mm) and a positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Multiple gallstones are visualized, the largest being 18 mm, one of which appears impacted in the gallbladder neck. Minimal pericholecystic fluid present. Common bile duct (CBD) measures 5 mm (normal). No intrahepatic biliary dilatation. Liver, spleen, and kidneys appear normal.
Therapy:
    • 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV
    • Spasmex (trospium) 0.02 mg IV
    • Ketorolac 30 mg IV
    • Ciprofloxacin 400 mg IV (Due to Penicillin allergy)
    • Metronidazole 500 mg IV
Conclusion:
The patient presents with clinical, laboratory, and sonographic evidence of acute calculous cholecystitis. Laboratory results show leukocytosis with a left shift and elevated CRP, consistent with an inflammatory process. Normal lipase rules out concomitant acute pancreatitis. CBD diameter is normal, suggesting no choledocholithiasis at this time. The patient was evaluated by the on-call abdominal surgeon. Admission to the Department of Surgery is indicated for further management and planned laparoscopic cholecystectomy. The patient remains hemodynamically stable, with pain partially relieved after analgesic therapy.

(Dr. Marko Ivić, 22.10.2024 03:45)

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1978

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
K81.0 Acute cholecystitis
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 112/68 mmHg; Pulse: 105/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 38.8°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15;
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Severe abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting.
Anamnesis
History: Known cholelithiasis diagnosed 2 years ago via ultrasound, asymptomatic until now. No previous surgeries. No history of hypertension or diabetes.
Current Illness: Patient reports sudden onset of sharp, constant pain in the upper right quadrant of the abdomen starting approximately 6 hours ago. The pain radiates to the right shoulder and is associated with multiple episodes of non-bilious vomiting. Patient took 1000 mg of paracetamol at home with no relief. Reports feeling "chilled" and shaky.
Functions: Appetite poor due to nausea. Last bowel movement yesterday, normal. Urination normal, though darker than usual.
Habits: Non-smoker, occasional social alcohol consumption.
Medications: None regularly.
Allergies: Penicillin (reported skin rash).
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build and nourishment, conscious, communicative, oriented. Appears in acute distress due to pain. Febrile (38.8°C), tachycardic, eupneic at rest. Skin is warm and dry, no jaundice noted in the sclera.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no rales or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs.
Abdomen: Slightly distended. Extremely tender on palpation in the right upper quadrant (RUQ). Positive Murphy's sign (inspiratory arrest on deep palpation of the RUQ). Guarding present in the upper abdomen. No rebound tenderness. Audible but diminished peristalsis. Lumbar percussion painless.
Extremities: Symmetrical, no edema, palpable peripheral pulses.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 106/min, normal axis, no ST-T wave abnormalities.
Abdominal Ultrasound:
Gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall measuring 5.5 mm. Presence of several gallstones, the largest measuring 18 mm, one of which appears impacted in the gallbladder neck. Significant pericholecystic fluid collection noted. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. Common bile duct measures 4 mm (normal). Intrahepatic bile ducts are not dilated.
(Dr. Marko Horvat, 18.07.2024 21:15)
Laboratory Results:
WBC 18.5 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 148 g/L, Hct 0.44 L/L, MCV 89.8 fL, MCH 30.2 pg, Platelets 210 [1e9]/L, NEU% 84.2 %, LYM% 10.1 %, NEU # 15.58 [1e9]/L, CRP 142.0 mg/L, Glucose 6.2 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 82 µmol/L, AST 35 U/L, ALT 38 U/L, GGT 45 U/L, ALP 92 U/L, Total Bilirubin 18 µmol/L, Amylase 62 U/L, Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.0 mmol/L.
(Dr. Ivana Babić, 18.07.2024 21:40)
Therapy
1000 ml 0.9% NaCl IV
Ketonal 100 mg IV
Reglan 10 mg IV
Controloc 40 mg IV
Conclusion
Clinical and radiological findings are consistent with acute calculous cholecystitis. The patient is systemic inflammatory response syndrome (SIRS) positive (fever, tachycardia, leukocytosis). Surgical consultation obtained (Dr. Perić). Patient is scheduled for urgent admission to the Department of Surgery for further management and potential laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
Hemodynamically stable but requires intensive monitoring and pain management.
gold_reference: The patient presents with acute cholecystitis and clear signs of systemic infection/SIRS (fever of 38.8°C, WBC of 18.5, and CRP of 142). Despite these findings, the physician failed to order or administer intravenous antibiotics. In the context of acute cholecystitis with systemic signs, early antibiotic therapy is a critical standard of care to prevent progression to gallbladder gangrene, perforation, or sepsis.

Patient 4
MBOO: 294810572

Date of Birth: 1962

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
I26.9 Pulmonary embolism without mention of acute cor pulmonale
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; Blood Pressure: 106/68 mmHg; Pulse: 118/min; SpO2: 91%; Temperature: 36.7°C; VAS: 6-Moderate Pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Anamnesis
Personal Anamnesis: Long-standing hypertension, treated. Ten days ago, underwent elective arthroscopic meniscectomy of the right knee. Postoperative course was described as normal; patient was mobilizing with crutches.
Current Illness: Patient presents to the ED due to the sudden onset of sharp, right-sided pleuritic chest pain that began approximately 3 hours ago while resting. The pain is accompanied by significant shortness of breath and a dry cough. He denies any calf pain or swelling, though he notes the right knee is still slightly bruised from surgery. No history of palpitations, syncope, or hemoptysis.
Functions: Appetite normal, no dysuria, regular bowel movements.
Habits: Former smoker (quit 5 years ago), social alcohol consumption.
Medications: Amlopin 5 mg 1+0+0, Andol 100 mg 1+0+0.
Drug Allergies: Denies.
Status
Patient is of medium build, alert, and oriented. Appears mildly distressed, tachypneic at rest. Skin is pale but not cyanotic. No peripheral edema noted; right knee surgical sites are clean and healing.
Lungs: Tachypneic, respiratory rate 24/min. Auscultation reveals clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing. SpO2 91% on room air, improves to 97% on 2L/min O2 via nasal cannula.
Heart: Tachycardic, rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs or S3/S4. BP 105/65 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no organomegaly, bowel sounds present.
Extremities: Right knee shows mild postoperative ecchymosis, no calf tenderness, Homan’s sign negative. Palpable peripheral pulses.
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests
ECG: Sinus tachycardia, heart rate 116/min. Right axis deviation. S1Q3T3 pattern present. No ST-segment elevation.
Lab Results:
WBC 11.2 [1e9]/L, RBC 4.90 [1e12]/L, HGB 152 g/L, Hct 0.441 L/L, MCV 90.0 fL, Platelets 245 [1e9]/L, NEU% 72.0 %, PV-share 1.10, APTT 24.2 s, D-Dimer 2840 ng/mL [ref <500], Urea 7.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, AST 34 U/L, ALT 31 U/L, LDH 210 U/L, CRP 14.5 mg/L, hs-TNT 48.2 ng/L [ref <14], Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L.
(LUKOVIĆ MARKO, Medical Biochemist, 18.07.2024 09:45)

Radiographic Imaging:
Chest X-ray (AP):
Lungs are clear of focal infiltrates. No pleural effusion. Heart shadow is within normal limits. Diaphragm positions are normal.
(Dr. Ivana Perić, 18.07.2024 10:15)

MSCT Angiography of Pulmonary Arteries:
Contrast-enhanced CT shows a clear filling defect in the right lower lobe segmental artery and a smaller defect in the right middle lobe branch, consistent with acute pulmonary embolism. No evidence of major central thrombus. No significant right ventricular enlargement (RV/LV ratio 0.9). No pulmonary infarction or pleural effusion.
(Dr. Marija Ćavar Borić, 18.07.2024 11:30)

Therapy
- Clecane 80 mg (0.8 ml) SC administered at 11:45
- 500 ml 0.9% NaCl IV
- Oxygen 2L/min via nasal cannula

Conclusion
The patient has a confirmed acute segmental pulmonary embolism following recent orthopedic surgery. Clinically, the patient has responded well to initial oxygen therapy and a bolus of IV fluids, with BP stabilizing at 110/70 mmHg. Although the troponin is mildly elevated, the CT does not show right ventricular strain. 
The patient is assessed as low risk for complications based on the segmental nature of the clot.
Decision: Discharge home with oral anticoagulant therapy.
Therapy: Xarelto 15 mg 2x1 for 21 days, then 20 mg 1x1.
Follow-up: Report to primary care physician in 48 hours. Return to ED immediately if shortness of breath worsens or syncope occurs.

(Dr. Ante Kovač, 18.07.2024 13:20)
gold_reference: The patient meets several criteria for high-risk or intermediate-risk pulmonary embolism that necessitate hospital admission. Specifically, the patient is hypoxic (SpO2 91% on room air), tachycardic (HR 118), and has an elevated troponin (48.2 ng/L), indicating myocardial strain. According to the Pulmonary Embolism Severity Index (PESI) or simplified PESI, this patient is at high risk for 30-day mortality. Discharging a hypoxic patient with evidence of cardiac strain and recent surgery is unsafe and deviates from standard clinical guidelines (e.g., ESC or ACCP guidelines).

report_content:
Date of birth: 1949

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
I48.0 Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation
I10 Essential (primary) hypertension
E11.9 Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 158/94 mmHg; Pulse: 122/min; SpO2: 96%; GCS: 15; Temp: 36.7°C
Reason for visit
Palpitations and shortness of breath
History
From the history: The patient is a 75-year-old female with a known history of hypertension and type 2 diabetes mellitus, managed with oral therapy. She has no history of previous cardiac arrhythmias or stroke.
Current illness: Symptoms began suddenly this morning around 08:00 while she was having breakfast. She describes a "racing heart" and a sensation of "fluttering" in the chest, accompanied by mild dyspnea on exertion. She denies syncope, focal neurological deficits, or chest pain.
Family history: Non-contributory.
Habits: Non-smoker, does not consume alcohol.
Functions: Normal appetite, no weight loss, normal bowel and bladder function.
Medications: Ramipril 5 mg 1x1, Metformin 850 mg 2x1, Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (rash).
Status
Patient is conscious, oriented, and communicative. Of medium build, eupneic at rest, but becomes slightly tachypneic when moving to the examination table. Skin is warm and dry, no peripheral cyanosis. Neck veins not distended.
Chest: Symmetrical, normal respiratory excursions.
Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally, no rales or wheezing. SpO2 96% on room air.
Heart: Irregularly irregular rhythm, tachycardic. Heart sounds are clear, no audible murmurs or gallops. Peripheral pulses are irregular and unequal in volume.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, no palpable masses or organomegaly. Peristalsis present.
Limbs: No pretibial edema, no signs of deep vein thrombosis.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Atrial fibrillation with a ventricular rate of 118-125/min. Intermediate electrical axis. No ST-segment elevation or depression. No pathological Q waves.
Laboratory Results:
    • WBC 7.8 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.42 [10¹²/L], Hb 132 g/L, Hct 0.401 L/L, MCV 90.7 fL, Platelets 245 [10⁹/L]
    • Glucose 9.4 mmol/L (elevated), Urea 7.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 58 mL/min/1.73m²
    • Na 138 mmol/L, K 4.2 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L
    • Troponin T (hs-TnT) 9.2 ng/L (within normal limits), CRP 3.4 mg/L
    • TSH 1.45 mIU/L (normal)
(Dr. MARIĆ IVAN, Clinical Biochemist, 22.10.2024 10:45)
Chest X-ray:
No acute pleuropulmonary changes. Transverse diameter of the heart is at the upper limit of normal. No signs of pulmonary congestion.
(Dr. FILIPOVIĆ ANA, MD, 22.10.2024 11:10)
Conclusion
The patient presented with new-onset symptomatic atrial fibrillation. During the stay in the emergency department, heart rate was partially controlled with a trial of Bisoprolol 2.5 mg. No evidence of acute myocardial infarction or electrolyte imbalance was found.
Based on the clinical assessment, the CHA2DS2-VASc score was calculated as 2 (1 point for Age > 65 and 1 point for Female sex). Given that the score is 2 in a female patient and this is a first-time detected episode, the risk of thromboembolism is considered low-intermediate. Therefore, oral anticoagulation is not indicated at this time.
The patient is discharged with a recommendation to start Bisoprolol 2.5 mg 1x1.
Mandatory follow-up with a cardiologist for an echocardiogram and consideration of rhythm vs. rate control strategy.
In case of chest pain, fainting, or speech difficulties, report immediately to the emergency department.
gold_reference: The clinician miscalculated the CHA2DS2-VASc score. The patient is 75 years old (2 points), female (1 point), has hypertension (1 point), and has diabetes (1 point). The correct total score is 5, not 2. A score of 5 represents a high risk for stroke, making anticoagulation strongly indicated.

---

## Imaging

### Ultrasound Abdomen (16:05)
Appendix not reliably visualized due to bowel gas. Small amount of free fluid in RLQ. No hydronephrosis.

### CT Abdomen/Pelvis with IV Contrast (16:55)
**Findings/Impression:**  
Dilated appendix up to 11 mm with wall thickening and periappendiceal fat stranding. Small periappendiceal fluid. No definite abscess. No free air. Findings consistent with **acute uncomplicated appendicitis**.

---

## Consultations
**General Surgery consulted (17:10):**  
Agrees with diagnosis. Plan for urgent laparoscopic appendectomy this evening.

---

## ED Therapy / Interventions
- IV access x1, monitoring  
- **0.9% NaCl 1000 mL IV** bolus then maintenance  
- **Paracetamol 1 g IV**  
- **Ondansetron 4 mg IV**  
- **Morphine 2 mg IV**, repeated once (total 4 mg) with pain decrease to 4/10  

**ERROR (documented):** *“No antibiotics were ordered in the ED; patient sent to Surgery for appendectomy preparation.”*

---

## ED Course
After fluids and analgesia, tachycardia improved (112 → 100/min). Temperature trended down. Persistent RLQ tenderness. No hypotension, no peritonitis beyond localized guarding. Patient transferred to surgical unit for operative management.

---

## Disposition
**Admit to General Surgery** for urgent laparoscopic appendectomy.

---

## Conclusion / Discharge-from-ED Plan (to inpatient team)
Clinical picture, labs (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and CT confirm **acute uncomplicated appendicitis**. Patient stabilized with IV fluids, antiemetic, and analgesia and transferred to Surgery for operative treatment.

---
gold_reference: In suspected/confirmed acute appendicitis, preoperative broad-spectrum IV antibiotics are standard to reduce postoperative infectious complications and cover likely enteric organisms. Omitting antibiotics before surgery is a clinically significant gap in care.

report_content:
Date of birth: 1975

Triage category: 3
Diagnoses
K81.0 Acute cholecystitis
K80.2 Gallstones (calculus) of gallbladder without cholecystitis
Triage Data
Triage category: 3; BP: 142/88 mmHg; Pulse: 92/min; SpO2: 99%; Temp: 37.9°C; VAS: 8 - Severe pain; GCS: 15
Reason for visit
Severe abdominal pain, nausea
History
From the history: Patient has a history of gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and obesity (BMI 32). No previous abdominal surgeries.
Family history: Mother had a cholecystectomy at age 50.
Current illness: The patient reports sudden onset of sharp, constant pain in the upper right quadrant of the abdomen starting approximately 6 hours ago, shortly after a heavy dinner. The pain radiates to the right shoulder blade. She reports significant nausea and two episodes of non-projectile vomiting of gastric contents. She denies diarrhea, constipation, or urinary symptoms. She notes that the pain is not relieved by over-the-counter antacids.
Habits: Non-smoker, occasional social alcohol consumption (1-2 glasses of wine per week).
Functions: Appetite currently absent due to nausea, urination normal, last bowel movement yesterday (normal consistency).
Medications: Pantoprazole 20 mg 1x1, occasional Ibuprofen for headaches.
Drug allergies: Penicillin (reports rash).
Status
Of medium musculoskeletal build, increased adipose tissue, oriented, distressed due to pain, eupneic at rest, subfebrile, anicteric, skin dry and warm, no peripheral edema.
Chest: Symmetrical, normal respiratory excursions.
Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Rhythmic action, clear tones, no murmurs. BP: 140/85 mmHg. Pulse: 94/min.
Abdomen: Above the level of the chest, soft, significantly tender on deep palpation in the right hypochondrium. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound tenderness (Blumberg sign negative). No palpable masses or organomegaly. Bowel sounds are present but diminished.
Limbs: Symmetrical, no edema, peripheral pulses palpable and equal.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
ECG: Sinus rhythm, heart rate 92/min, normal axis, no acute ST-T changes.
Laboratory Results:
WBC 13.8 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.62 [10¹²/L], Hb 138 g/L, Hct 0.410 L/L, MCV 88.7 fL, Platelets 245 [10⁹/L], NEU% 78.2%, LYM% 14.5%, MONO% 6.8%, EOS% 0.3%, BASO% 0.2%, NEU # 10.79 [10⁹/L], Glucose 5.8 mmol/L, Urea 4.9 mmol/L, Creatinine 72 µmol/L, eGFR 92 mL/min/1.73m², AST 24 U/L, ALT 28 U/L, GGT 35 U/L, ALP 92 U/L, Total Bilirubin 15.2 µmol/L, Amylase - serum 48 U/L, Lipase 32 U/L, CRP 32.4 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
(Ivan Horvat, M.Sc. Biochemist, 18.07.2024 20:45)

Abdominal Ultrasound:
The liver is of normal size and structure, without focal lesions. The gallbladder is distended, with a thickened and edematous wall (thickness 4.8 mm), showing a "double-contour" sign. Several calculus shadows are visualized within the lumen, the largest measuring 18 mm, one of which appears impacted in the gallbladder neck. Positive sonographic Murphy's sign. The common bile duct (CBD) is not dilated (4 mm). No pericholecystic fluid collection seen. The pancreas and spleen appear normal. No free fluid in the abdominal cavity.
(Dr. MARIĆ IVANA, MD, 18.07.2024 21:15)

Therapy Administered in ED
- Saline 0.9% 500 ml IV
- Spasmex (trospium chloride) 0.02 mg IV
- Torecan (thiethylperazine) 6.5 mg IV
- Ketonal (ketoprofen) 100 mg IV
- Controloc (pantoprazole) 40 mg IV

Conclusion
The patient presents with clinical, laboratory (leukocytosis, elevated CRP), and ultrasonographic signs of acute calculous cholecystitis. Liver enzymes and lipase are within reference ranges, making choledocholithiasis and biliary pancreatitis less likely. After initial analgesic and antiemetic therapy, the patient reports moderate improvement in pain (VAS 4). 
A surgical consultation was performed (Dr. Petrović). Based on the findings, the patient is indicated for admission to the Department of General Surgery for further monitoring and laparoscopic cholecystectomy.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

________________________________________

•	VAS: 9 – Severe pain  
•	Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 15  
________________________________________  
History  
Present Illness:  
•	Onset ~3 hours prior to arrival: sudden, severe right-sided flank pain, colicky, coming in waves, radiating to the right lower abdomen and groin.  
•	Associated symptoms: nausea and one episode of vomiting at home.  
•	Reports urinary urgency and mild dysuria since the pain started; notes urine looked “slightly pink.”  
•	Denies fever, chills, chest pain, shortness of breath, syncope, diarrhea, or testicular pain.  
•	No recent trauma. No similar episode previously.

Medical History:  
•	Hypertension.  
•	Hyperlipidemia.  
•	No prior kidney stones known. No chronic kidney disease.

Surgical History:  
•	Appendectomy (childhood).

Medications:  
•	Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1  
•	Atorvastatin 20 mg 1x1  
•	No anticoagulants/antiplatelets.

Allergies:  
•	No known drug allergies.

Habits / Social:  
•	Non-smoker. Rare alcohol use.  
•	Works as an office administrator.

Functions:  
•	Last bowel movement yesterday, normal.  
•	Urine output preserved; current urinary symptoms as above.  
________________________________________  
Physical Examination  
•	General: In significant pain, restless, oriented x3, speaking full sentences, well-perfused, no respiratory distress.  
•	HEENT: Mucosa moist.  
•	Neck: Supple.  
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheeze/crackles.  
•	Heart: Regular rhythm, no murmurs.  
•	Abdomen: Soft, mild tenderness in right lower quadrant without guarding or rebound. No palpable masses.  
•	CVA tenderness: Marked right costovertebral angle tenderness; left CVA non-tender.  
•	Extremities: No edema; peripheral pulses palpable and symmetric.  
•	Neuro: No focal deficits.

Repeat vitals after initial analgesia (approx. 60 min):  
•	BP 142/86 mmHg, Pulse 82/min, SpO2 99%, Temp 36.8 °C, VAS 3/10.  
________________________________________  
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests  

ECG (performed due to severe pain and hypertension on arrival):  
•	Sinus rhythm, HR 92/min, no ischemic ST-T changes.

Laboratory Findings (reported 06.02.2026, 03:10):  
•	CBC:  
o	WBC 9.8 ×10⁹/L  
o	Hemoglobin 148 g/L  
o	Platelets 236 ×10⁹/L  
•	CRP: 3 mg/L  
•	Basic metabolic panel:  
o	Na 140 mmol/L  
o	K 4.2 mmol/L  
o	Cl 103 mmol/L  
o	Urea 6.7 mmol/L  
o	Creatinine 86 µmol/L (eGFR > 90 mL/min/1.73m²)  
o	Glucose 6.1 mmol/L  
•	Liver enzymes: within normal limits  
•	Lipase: 28 U/L (normal range)  

Urinalysis:  
•	Blood: 3+  
•	RBC: 50–100 /HPF  
•	WBC: 0–5 /HPF  
•	Nitrite: negative  
•	Leukocyte esterase: negative  
•	Protein: trace  
•	Specific gravity: 1.020  
•	pH: 6.0  

Imaging:  
•	Non-contrast CT abdomen/pelvis (renal colic protocol):  
o	3 mm calculus in the distal right ureter near the ureterovesical junction.  
o	Mild right hydroureter and mild hydronephrosis.  
o	No perinephric stranding suggestive of infection.  
o	No other acute abdominal pathology identified.  
(Reviewed with radiology; timestamp 06.02.2026, 03:55)  
________________________________________  
Therapy (ED Course)  
•	IV access, monitoring.  
•	Analgesia: Ketorolac 30 mg IV once.  
•	Antiemetic: Ondansetron 4 mg IV once.  
•	Hydration: 0.9% NaCl 500 mL IV.  
•	After imaging confirmation and symptom improvement:  
o	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO given in ED (medical expulsive therapy).  

Response: Pain decreased from VAS 9/10 to 3/10; nausea resolved. Hemodynamically stable throughout observation. No fever. Able to tolerate oral fluids prior to discharge.  
________________________________________  
Conclusion / Disposition  
Assessment:  
•	Clinical presentation, urinalysis (hematuria without signs of infection), and CT confirm uncomplicated right distal ureteral stone (3 mm) with mild hydronephrosis.  
•	No evidence of obstructive pyelonephritis (afebrile, normal CRP, no pyuria, nitrite negative, no systemic toxicity). Renal function preserved.

Discharge Plan and Recommendations:  
•	Outpatient management appropriate due to symptom control, stable vitals, preserved renal function, and no infection.  
•	Medications:  
o	Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (take with food; avoid other NSAIDs concurrently).  
o	Paracetamol 1,000 mg PO every 8 hours as needed (max 3,000 mg/day if self-medicating).  
o	Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO once daily for up to 14 days (stop earlier if stone passes and symptoms resolve; caution for dizziness/orthostasis).  
o	Ondansetron 4 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for nausea.  
•	Advise oral hydration (drink to maintain light-yellow urine; avoid forced excessive fluids if nauseated).  
•	Strain urine to capture stone for analysis.  
•	Urology follow-up within 7–10 days (earlier if persistent pain) and repeat imaging per urology to confirm passage if not observed.  

Return to ED / call EMS immediately for:  
•	Fever/chills, worsening flank pain not controlled with medication, persistent vomiting/inability to keep fluids down, decreased urination, fainting, or any new concerning symptoms.

Patient received verbal and written instructions and demonstrated understanding.  
________________________________________
gold_reference: No errors

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1968  

**Triage Category:** 2  

**Diagnoses**  
- N17.2 Acute kidney failure, stage 2 (acute kidney injury)  
- I50.22 Chronic systolic (congestive) heart failure with acute exacerbation  
- E87.5 Hyperkalemia  

---

### Triage Data  
Triage Cat: **2**; Blood Pressure: **168/96 mmHg**; Pulse: **112 /min**; SpO₂: **96 %**; Temp: **37.4 °C**; VAS: **4**; GCS: **15**  

---

### Vital Signs  

| Time (min) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) | Pain VAS |
|------------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------|-----------|----------|

| 60 | 158/90 | 100 | 20 | 97 | 37.2 | 2 |
| 120 | 152/88 | 96 | 18 | 98 | 37.1 | 1 |
| 240 | 150/86 | 94 | 18 | 98 | 37.0 | 0 |

*All values recorded on cardiac monitor; urine output measured via Foley catheter.*

---

### Reason for Arrival  
“I’ve not been able to pee much for the last three days and I feel really weak.”

---

### Anamnesis  

**History (PMH)**  
- Hypertension – on lisinopril 20 mg daily  
- Type 2 Diabetes Mellitus – metformin 1000 mg BID  
- Chronic Heart Failure, NYHA II (EF 45 %) – furosemide 40 mg daily  
- Stage 3 Chronic Kidney Disease (baseline Cr 1.0 mg/dL, eGFR ≈ 80 mL/min/1.73 m²)  
- Obstructive Sleep Apnea – CPAP nightly  

**Current Illness (HPI)**  
- **72 h prior:** Lifting a 30‑lb concrete block → low‑grade lumbar/back pain → self‑medicated with ibuprofen 800 mg q6h (≈ 3 g/day) for 4 days.  
- **48 h prior:** Nausea, intermittent non‑bloody vomiting; urine output markedly decreased (only a few drops per void). No dysuria, flank pain, or hematuria.  
- **24 h prior:** Bilateral ankle swelling, mild exertional dyspnea (climbing two flights of stairs). No chest pain, fever, cough.  
- **12 h prior:** Light‑headedness on standing; urine turned dark “tea‑colored.”  
- **6 h prior:** Oliguria < 200 mL/24 h, progressive generalized weakness → presented to ED.  
- Denies prior similar episodes, recent infections, or medication changes other than ibuprofen binge.

**Functions**  
- **Bowel:** Normal frequency, no constipation or diarrhea.  
- **Urination:** Oliguria (≈ 22 mL/h over first 4 h), dark tea‑colored urine, no dysuria, no hematuria.  

**Habits**  
- Tobacco: 1 pack‑year, quit 15 years ago.  
- Alcohol: 1–2 drinks/week (social).  
- Illicit drugs: None.  

**Medications** (all taken orally unless noted)  
- Lisinopril 20 mg PO daily  
- Metformin 1000 mg PO BID  
- Furosemide 40 mg PO daily  
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly  
- Ibuprofen 800 mg PO q6h PRN (taken 4 times in the past 24 h)  
- Albuterol inhaler 2 puffs PRN (mild asthma)  
- Calcium‑vitamin D 600 IU/400 mg PO daily  

**Allergies**  
- Penicillin → urticaria (hives)  
- Latex → contact dermatitis  

---

### Status (Physical Examination)  

| System | Findings |
|--------|----------|
| **General** | Alert, oriented ×3, mildly diaphoretic, dry oral mucosa, appears fatigued |
| **HEENT** | Dry tongue, no scleral icterus, pupils equal/reactive |
| **Neck** | No jugular venous distension beyond 8 cm H₂O (elevated) |
| **Cardiovascular** | Regular rate/rhythm, S1/S2 normal, no murmurs, no rubs, JVP 8 cm H₂O |
| **Respiratory** | Bibasilar crackles (2/6), no wheezes, clear to auscultation elsewhere |
| **Abdomen** | Soft, mildly distended, non‑tender, no CVA tenderness, normal bowel sounds |
| **Extremities** | 1+ pitting edema bilaterally at ankles, no calf tenderness, peripheral pulses palpable |
| **Neurologic** | No focal deficits, strength 5/5 in all extremities, mild tremor of hands (possible uremic) |
| **Skin** | No rash, no cellulitis, dry skin, no peripheral cyanosis |
| **Weight** | 102 kg (baseline 100 kg) – reported 2 kg gain over last 24 h |

---

### Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  

**ECG** – Sinus tachycardia 112 bpm; peaked T‑waves noted in leads V2‑V4; no ST‑segment changes.  

**Laboratory Results**  

| Test | Result | Units | Reference |
|------|--------|-------|-----------|
| **CBC** | | | |
| – Hemoglobin | 13.2 | g/dL | 13.5‑17.5 |
| – Hematocrit | 40.0 | % | 41‑53 |
| – WBC | 12.1 | ×10⁹/L | 4‑11 |
| – Neutrophils | 78 | % | 40‑70 |
| – Platelets | 210 | ×10⁹/L | 150‑400 |
| **Electrolytes & Metabolites** | | | |
| – Na⁺ | 138 | mmol/L | 135‑145 |
| – K⁺ | 5.9 | mmol/L | 3.5‑5.0 |
| – Cl⁻ | 102 | mmol/L | 98‑106 |
| – HCO₃⁻ | 18 | mmol/L | 22‑28 |
| – Glucose (random) | 172 | mg/dL | 70‑110 |
| – BUN | 55 | mg/dL | 7‑20 |
| – Creatinine | 3.2 | mg/dL | 0.8‑1.3 |
| – eGFR (CKD‑EPI) | 22 | mL/min/1.73 m² | ≥ 60 |
| – Calcium (corrected) | 8.7 | mg/dL | 8.5‑10.5 |
| – Phosphate | 4.5 | mg/dL | 2.5‑4.5 |
| – Magnesium | 2.0 | mg/dL | 1.7‑2.2 |
| – Albumin | 3.5 | g/dL | 3.5‑5.0 |
| – Total bilirubin | 0.5 | mg/dL | 0‑1.2 |
| – AST | 28 | U/L | 10‑40 |
| – ALT | 30 | U/L | 7‑56 |
| – Alkaline phosphatase | 78 | U/L | 44‑147 |
| – CK | 120 | U/L | 30‑200 |
| – CRP | 12 | mg/L | < 5 |
| – Lactate | 1.8 | mmol/L | 0.5‑2.0 |
| – Troponin I | 0.02 | ng/mL | ≤ 0.04 |
| **Arterial Blood Gas (room air)** | pH 7.30, pCO₂ 32 mmHg, pO₂ 80 mmHg, HCO₃⁻ 16 mmol/L, Base excess ‑8 mmol/L |
| **Urinalysis** | | | |
| – Specific gravity | 1.025 | – | 1.005‑1.030 |
| – pH | 6.0 | – | 5‑7 |
| – Protein | 2+ (≈ 300 mg/dL) | – | Negative |
| – Glucose | Negative | – | – |
| – Ketones | Negative | – | – |
| – Blood | Negative | – | – |
| – Leukocyte esterase | Negative | – | – |
| – Nitrite | Negative | – | – |
| – Microscopy: no RBCs, no WBCs, no casts | – | – | – |
| **Urine Sodium** | 45 | mmol/L | – |
| **Fractional Excretion of Na⁺ (FeNa)** | 4 % | – | > 2 % suggests intrinsic ATN |
| **Urine Output (first 4 h)** | 90 mL | – | ≈ 22 mL/h (oliguria) |
| **Renal Bedside Ultrasound** | Kidneys 11 cm, normal cortical thickness, no hydronephrosis, no stones | – | – |
| **Chest X‑ray (PA/Lateral)** | Mild interstitial edema, small bilateral pleural effusions, cardiomegaly (CTR ≈ 0.55) | – | – |

**Consults** – Nephrology consulted at 02:20 h for AKI management; Cardiology consulted at 02:45 h for HF exacerbation and hyper‑K⁺ evaluation.

---

### Therapy Administered in ED  

| Time | Intervention |
|------|--------------|
| 00:15 | Placed 16‑Fr Foley catheter – urine output monitoring initiated |
| 00:20 | Discontinued **lisinopril** and **ibuprofen** (held for renal protection) |
| 00:25 | Cardiac monitor attached; supplemental O₂ 2 L/min nasal cannula (maintained SpO₂ ≥ 96 %) |
| 00:30 | IV **Calcium gluconate 10 %**, 10 mL over 10 min (membrane stabilization) |
| 00:35 | IV **Regular insulin** 10 U + **Dextrose 50 %** 50 mL (K⁺ shift) |
| 00:40 | Nebulized **Albuterol** 2.5 mg over 10 min (additional K⁺ shift) |
| 00:45 | IV **Furosemide** 40 mg push (diuresis for volume overload) |
| 00:50 | IV **Sodium bicarbonate 8.4 %**, 50 mL over 5 min (address metabolic acidosis) |
| 00:55 | IV **Ondansetron** 4 mg push (anti‑emetic) |
| 01:00 | PO **Acetaminophen** 1 g (analgesia) |
| 01:05 | Small isotonic fluid challenge **0.9% NaCl** 250 mL over 30 min (cautious volume assessment) |
| 01:15 | Repeat BMP: K⁺ ↓ to 5.5 mmol/L, HCO₃⁻ ↑ to 19 mmol/L |
| 01:30 | Ongoing IV **Furosemide** 20 mg q6h as needed (based on urine output) |
| 02:00 | Education: medication review, avoidance of NSAIDs, importance of daily weight & fluid balance |
| 02:30 | **Discharge order placed – patient to be discharged home with close outpatient follow‑up for renal function, electrolytes, and fluid balance** |

---

### Conclusion  

**Clinical Summary**  
58‑year‑old male with known CKD stage 3, chronic systolic heart failure, and hypertension presented with a 3‑day history of oliguria, volume overload, and generalized weakness. Laboratory work‑up revealed AKI stage 2 (creatinine 3.2 mg/dL, eGFR 22 mL/min/1.73 m²), hyperkalemia (K⁺ 5.9 mmol/L) with peaked T‑waves, metabolic acidosis (pH 7.30, HCO₃⁻ 18 mmol/L), and signs of fluid overload (JVP 8 cm H₂O, bibasilar crackles, peripheral edema). Urinalysis and FeNa 4 % are consistent with intrinsic acute tubular necrosis, likely precipitated by NSAID‑induced renal vasoconstriction (“triple whammy” with ACE‑I and loop diuretic) superimposed on prerenal hypoperfusion from recent vomiting and HF decompensation.

**Disposition**  
- **Discharge home with close outpatient follow‑up for renal function, electrolytes, and fluid balance**  
- Continue IV furosemide with urine‑output‑guided titration; monitor daily weights.  
- Maintain potassium‑lowering regimen until K⁺ ≤ 5.0 mmol/L; repeat ECG after each intervention.  
- Serial BMP, ABG, and urine output q4‑6 h; consider emergent hemodialysis if K⁺ > 6.5 mmol/L, refractory acidosis (pH < 7.20), or worsening oliguria despite diuresis.  
- Hold ACE‑I and NSAIDs; re‑evaluate antihypertensive regimen once renal function stabilizes.  
- Optimize heart‑failure therapy (consider uptitration of guideline‑directed medical therapy after AKI resolves).  
- Provide patient education on medication avoidance (NSAIDs), importance of daily weight monitoring, and early return precautions (chest pain, dyspnea, worsening edema, palpitations).  
- Arrange follow‑up with primary care physician and nephrology within 1 week of discharge.  

**Attending Physician:** Dr. Ana Horvat, MD, Department of Emergency Medicine  
**Report Date:** 02 February 2026  

---
gold_reference: The patient meets clear admission criteria: AKI stage 2 with creatinine 3.2 mg/dL, hyperkalemia (K⁺ 5.9 mmol/L) with ECG changes, metabolic acidosis, and volume overload from heart‑failure exacerbation. Discharging him home exposes him to life‑threatening complications such as worsening hyperkalemia, arrhythmias, progression to severe AKI, pulmonary edema, and the need for emergent dialysis. Outpatient monitoring cannot safely replace the intensive observation, fluid management, and electrolyte control required in the hospital.

Control Troponin: hs-TNT 90.2 ng/L

D-Dimer: 6.14 mg/L

Urine Analysis: Appearance cloudy, yellow color, pH 5.5, specific gravity 1.018 kg/L, glucose negative, bilirubin negative, ketones 1+, hemoglobin 2+, protein 1+, urobilinogen normal, nitrites negative, leukocytes 3+, leukocytes in urine 47 per field of view, erythrocytes in urine 10 per field of view, rare squamous epithelial cells, some bacteria and mucus, some amorphous phosphates per field of view.
Standing Abdominal X-ray:
No definite radiographic signs of free air under diaphragm domes.
Distended stomach with air-liquid level. A few air-fluid levels in the small intestine are visible, without distension.

Intravenous Pyelogram (IVP):
No convincing mineral shadows suggestive of lithiasis in the urotract on the native urography.



Urologist Consultation:
Conclusion: Cardiologist evaluation recommended. Follow-up with urologist after cardiologist's opinion.
Cardiologist Consultation:
Conclusion: No clinical signs, no changes on ECG, and insignificant rise in hs-Troponin. No acute coronary event at present. No absolute contraindications for surgery from a cardiological standpoint.
Therapy
    • 500 ml of 0.9% NaCl IV.
 
Conclusion
Admission to Urology.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1962

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses:
    • K81.0 Acute cholecystitis
    • K80.20 Calculus of gallbladder without cholecystitis without obstruction
    • J45.901 Bronchial asthma with (acute) exacerbation
    • I10 Essential (primary) hypertension

Triage Data:
    • Triage Category: 3
    • Blood Pressure: 158/94 mmHg
    • Pulse: 114/min
    • SpO2: 94% (on room air)
    • Temperature: 37.9°C
    • VAS: 8 - Severe pain
    • GCS: 15

Vital Signs:
22.08.2024, 18:15: BP 158/94, Pulse 114, SpO2 94%, Temp 37.9, VAS 8.
22.08.2024, 20:30: BP 142/88, Pulse 108, SpO2 95%, Temp 37.6, VAS 4.

Reason for Visit:
Severe pain under the right rib cage, nausea, and vomiting.

Medical History:
The patient presents with a 6-hour history of escalating, sharp pain in the right upper quadrant (RUQ) radiating to the right shoulder blade. The pain started shortly after a heavy dinner. She reports three episodes of non-bilious, non-bloody vomiting.
Chronic conditions:
1. Bronchial Asthma: Diagnosed 15 years ago. Frequent use of rescue inhalers. Hospitalized twice in the last three years for acute exacerbations. Currently reports mild wheezing and chest tightness.
2. Arterial Hypertension: Diagnosed 2010.
3. Cholelithiasis: Known gallstones for 5 years; previously asymptomatic.
4. Osteoarthritis: Primarily affecting the knees.

Habits:
    • Former smoker (quit 5 years ago).
    • Occasional alcohol consumption (1-2 glasses of wine per week).

Therapy:
    • Symbicort (Budesonide/Formoterol) 160/4.5 mcg 2 puffs twice daily.
    • Ventolin (Albuterol) as needed.
    • Lisinopril 10 mg 1x1.
    • Amlodipine 5 mg 1x1.
    • Meloxicam 15 mg as needed for joint pain.

Allergies:
Penicillin (hives).

Status:
    • General: Conscious, oriented, appears in acute distress due to pain. Slightly tachypneic. No icterus of the sclera.
    • Chest: Symmetrical. Lungs: Bilateral expiratory wheezing noted on auscultation, prolonged expiratory phase. SpO2 94% on room air.
    • Heart: Tachycardic rhythm, regular, clear tones, no murmurs. BP 158/94 mmHg.
    • Abdomen: Soft, severely tender in the RUQ. Positive Murphy's sign. No rebound tenderness or guarding. Bowel sounds present but diminished.
    • Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 115/min, normal axis, no ST-T segment abnormalities.
    • Laboratory Results:
      WBC 14.2 x10^9/L (Elevated), RBC 4.55 x10^12/L, HGB 138 g/L, HCT 0.412 L/L, Platelets 285 x10^9/L, NEU 78% (Elevated), CRP 62.4 mg/L (Elevated).
      Glucose 6.4 mmol/L, Urea 5.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 74 µmol/L, eGFR 82 mL/min/1.73m².
      AST 48 U/L, ALT 55 U/L, GGT 42 U/L, ALP 105 U/L, Total Bilirubin 19 µmol/L, Amylase 65 U/L.
      Na 139 mmol/L, K 3.9 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
    • Abdominal Ultrasound:
      Gallbladder is distended with a thickened wall (4.8 mm) and a small amount of pericholecystic fluid. Multiple gallstones are visualized, the largest being 14 mm, lodged in the neck of the gallbladder. The common bile duct is not dilated (4 mm). Positive sonographic Murphy's sign.
    • Chest X-ray:
      No signs of acute pneumonia or pneumothorax. Hyperinflation of the lungs consistent with history of asthma.

Therapy Administered in ED:
    • 0.9% NaCl 500 ml IV infusion.
    • Pantoprazole 40 mg IV.
    • Reglan (Metoclopramide) 10 mg IV.
    • Spasmex (Trospium) 5 mg IV.
    • Ketoprofen 100 mg IV (slow infusion).
    • **Propranolol 20 mg p.o. once to address persistent sinus tachycardia and associated anxiety.**

Conclusion:
Senior surgical and medical services consulted. Clinical presentation, elevated inflammatory markers, and ultrasound findings are consistent with Acute Cholecystitis. The patient also exhibits signs of a mild asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by the stress of the acute illness.
Surgical service recommends admission for IV antibiotics and laparoscopic cholecystectomy within 24-48 hours.
Condition upon transfer: Stable, pain significantly reduced (VAS 4), heart rate decreased to 104/min, but patient reports slight increase in "chest tightness."

Dr. Elena Marković, MD
Emergency Medicine Resident

Dr. Stjepan Horvat, MD
Senior Attending Physician
gold_reference: The patient has a documented history of severe bronchial asthma and was presenting with active wheezing and lower SpO2 (94%). Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker that is strictly contraindicated in patients with asthma as it can block beta-2 receptors in the lungs, leading to severe, potentially life-threatening bronchospasm.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1969

Triage Category: 2
________________________________________
Diagnoses:
•	I26.9 Pulmonary embolism without acute cor pulmonale
________________________________________
Triage Data:
•	Triage Category: 2
•	Blood Pressure (BP): 138/88 mmHg
•	Pulse: 108/min
•	SpO2: 91% on room air
•	Respirations: 24/min
•	VAS: 6 - Moderate to severe pain
•	Glasgow Coma Scale (GCS): 15
________________________________________
History
Medical History:
•	Hypertension, managed with Ramipril.
•	2018: Deep Vein Thrombosis (DVT) in left leg following a long-haul flight; treated with anticoagulants for 6 months.
•	2 weeks ago: Arthroscopic surgery on the right knee for meniscal repair. Currently non-weight bearing on crutches.
•	Ex-smoker (quit 10 years ago).
•	No known allergies.

Present Illness:
•	Patient presents with sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp, pleuritic chest pain on the right side, starting approximately 2 hours ago while resting.
•	Reports feeling "lightheaded" upon standing.
•	Denies cough, hemoptysis, or fever.
•	Noticed increased swelling in the right calf since yesterday, attributed to post-op recovery.

Functions:
•	Normal bowel movements, no melena.
•	Urination normal.

Habits:
•	Sedentary lifestyle due to recent surgery. Occasional alcohol consumption.

Medications:
•	Ramipril 5 mg 1x1
•	Ibuprofen 400 mg prn for knee pain
________________________________________
Physical Examination
•	General Status: Alert, anxious, tachypneic at rest. Pale skin, no cyanosis.
•	Lungs: Clear breath sounds bilaterally, no wheezing or crackles. Reduced air entry on the right base due to splinting from pain.
•	Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 audible, loud P2. No murmurs.
•	Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, bowel sounds present.
•	Extremities: Right leg in post-op brace. Significant pitting edema noted in the right calf and ankle. Right calf is warm and tender to palpation, significantly larger circumference than left. Peripheral pulses palpable.
•	Risk Stratification: Wells Score for Pulmonary Embolism calculated as 1.5 (Low Probability).
________________________________________
Diagnostic and Consultative Tests

•	ECG: Sinus tachycardia at 106 bpm. S1Q3T3 pattern noted (S wave in lead I, Q wave in lead III, Inverted T wave in lead III).

•	Laboratory Findings:
o	WBC: 11.2 ×10⁹/L
o	Hemoglobin: 145 g/L
o	Platelets: 280 ×10⁹/L
o	CRP: 12 mg/L
o	Troponin I: Negative
o	D-Dimer: 4500 ng/mL (High)
o	Creatinine: 95 µmol/L (eGFR > 60)
o	Reported by Dr. A. Petrovic (14.10.2024, 10:15).

•	Imaging (CT Pulmonary Angiogram):
o	Contrast opacification of the pulmonary arterial tree is good.
o	Large filling defect seen in the right main pulmonary artery extending into the lobar branches. Smaller segmental emboli noted in the left lower lobe.
o	Right ventricle appears mildly dilated (RV/LV ratio > 0.9). No consolidation or pleural effusion.
o	Conclusion: Bilateral Pulmonary Emboli with signs of right heart strain.
________________________________________
Therapy
•	Oxygen 3L via nasal cannula (SpO2 improved to 96%).
•	Enoxaparin 80 mg SC stat.
•	Paracetamol 1g IV.
________________________________________
Conclusion
•	Patient admitted to the Intensive Care Unit for monitoring and anticoagulation therapy.
•	Hemodynamically stable but requires close observation due to RV strain.
•	Vascular surgery consultation requested regarding right lower limb DVT management.
gold_reference: The Wells Score was miscalculated. The patient has signs of DVT (3.0 points), heart rate > 100 (1.5 points), surgery in the last 4 weeks (1.5 points), and a history of DVT (1.5 points). Even without the subjective 'PE is #1 diagnosis' criteria, the objective score is 7.5, which indicates High Probability, not 1.5 (Low Probability).

report_content:
**Date of Birth:** 1968  

**Triage Category:** 1  

---

### Diagnoses  
- **I16.0** – Hypertensive emergency, malignant (with encephalopathy)  
- **G93.5** – Cerebral edema (Posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome – PRES)  
- **N18.3** – Chronic kidney disease, stage 3 (moderate)  
- **E11.9** – Type 2 diabetes mellitus without complications  
- **I10** – Essential (primary) hypertension  
- **I25.2** – Old myocardial infarction, subsequent (history)  

---

### Triage Data  
**Triage Cat:** 1; **Blood Pressure:** 242/138 mmHg; **Pulse:** 112 /min; **SpO₂:** 96 %; **Temp:** 37.2 °C; **VAS:** 9; **GCS:** 13  

---

### Vital Signs  

| Time (hh:mm) | BP (mmHg) | HR (bpm) | RR (breaths/min) | SpO₂ (%) | Temp (°C) | GCS |
|--------------|-----------|----------|------------------|----------|-----------|-----|
| 09:12 | 242/138 | 112 | 20 | 96 | 37.2 | 13 |
| 09:20 | 235/130 | 110 | 20 | 96 | 37.2 | 13 |
| 09:45 | 230/128 | 108 | 20 | 96 | 37.3 | 13 |
| 10:10 | 210/115 | 105 | 20 | 96 | 37.3 | 13 |
| 10:30 | 180/100 | 100 | 18 | 97 | 37.2 | 14 (oriented to time/place) |

*All vitals obtained via automated cuff, cardiac monitor, and tympanic thermometer. BP measured on right arm, repeated twice at each time point.*

---

### Reason for Arrival  
“Severe throbbing headache, blurry vision, and confusion for the past 3 hours; self‑measured blood pressure ≈ 240/138 mmHg.”

---

### Anamnesis  

**History (PMH):**  
- Essential hypertension, diagnosed 12 y ago (poorly controlled).  
- Chronic kidney disease – stage 3b (baseline Cr 1.8 mg/dL, eGFR ≈ 35 mL/min).  
- Type 2 diabetes mellitus on metformin (HbA1c 8.2 %).  
- Hyperlipidemia on atorvastatin 40 mg daily.  
- Obstructive sleep apnea (CPAP prescribed, inconsistent use).  
- Myocardial infarction 5 y ago, treated with PCI (RCA stent).  

**Current Illness (HPI):**  
- **Onset:** 3 h ago while reviewing blueprints at the job‑site office.  
- **Headache:** Bifrontal, throbbing, 10/10, unchanged since onset; unrelieved by rest or acetaminophen.  
- **Visual changes:** New blurry vision with halos around lights.  
- **Nausea/Vomiting:** Nausea constant; one episode of non‑bloody emesis 1 h ago.  
- **Neurologic:** Light‑sensitivity, mild confusion (oriented to person only). No focal weakness, dysarthria, or seizures reported.  
- **Other:** No chest pain, dyspnea, palpitations, fever, or recent trauma.  
- **Self‑BP:** Measured 240/138 mmHg with digital cuff; missed lisinopril for 4 days due to lack of refills.  

**Functions:**  
- Bowel movements: regular, no constipation or diarrhea.  
- Urination: normal frequency, no dysuria or hematuria.  

**Habits:**  
- Tobacco: former smoker, quit 8 y ago, 10 pack‑year history.  
- Alcohol: 2–3 drinks per week, social.  
- Illicit drugs: none.  

**Medications (as of 02‑Feb‑2026):**  
- Lisinopril 40 mg PO daily (missed last 4 days)  
- Metoprolol succinate 100 mg PO daily  
- Amlodipine 10 mg PO daily  
- Metformin 1000 mg PO BID  
- Atorvastatin 40 mg PO nightly  
- Aspirin 81 mg PO daily  
- CPAP (inconsistent use)  
- **PRN** Ibuprofen 400 mg PO q6 h (last dose 2 h ago)  

**Allergies:**  
- Penicillin – urticarial rash  
- Latex – contact dermatitis  

---

### Status (Physical Examination)  

| System | Findings |
|--------|----------|
| **General** | Alert but mildly confused; oriented to person only, not time/place. |
| **HEENT** | Pupils 4 mm, equal, reactive to light. Fundoscopy: grade III papilledema with blurred disc margins; no retinal hemorrhages. No neck stiffness. |
| **Cardiovascular** | Regular rate and rhythm, S1/S2 normal, no murmurs, gallops, or rubs. Bounding peripheral pulses. |
| **Respiratory** | Clear bilateral breath sounds, no rales, wheezes, or use of accessory muscles. |
| **Abdomen** | Soft, non‑tender, non‑distended; no hepatosplenomegaly. |
| **Neurologic** | No focal deficits; cranial nerves II‑XII intact. Motor strength 5/5 in all extremities. Sensation intact. NIHSS = 1 (confusion). No asterixis. |
| **Extremities** | No edema, cyanosis, or clubbing. |
| **Skin** | No rashes, no flushing. |
| **Other** | No signs of acute pulmonary edema; capillary refill < 2 s. |

---

### Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations  

**ECG (09:45 AM):** Sinus tachycardia (HR 112 bpm); left ventricular hypertrophy by voltage criteria; nonspecific ST‑T changes; no acute ST‑segment elevation or new Q‑waves.  

**Laboratory Results (drawn 09:30 AM, reported 10:00 AM):**  

| Test | Result | Reference |
|------|--------|-----------|
| **CBC** | Hgb 13.8 g/dL; Hct 41 %; WBC 9.2 ×10⁹/L; Plt 210 ×10⁹/L | Normal |
| **BMP** | Na⁺ 138 mmol/L; K⁺ 4.9 mmol/L; Cl⁻ 102 mmol/L; HCO₃⁻ 24 mmol/L; BUN 32 mg/dL; Creatinine 2.2 mg/dL (↑ from baseline 1.8) | ↑ Cr |
| **Glucose** | 158 mg/dL (fasting) | ↑ |
| **Troponin I** | 0.08 ng/mL (reference < 0.04) | Mild elevation |
| **CK‑MB** | 1.5 ng/mL (reference < 5) | Normal |
| **Lactate** | 1.6 mmol/L (0.5‑2.2) | Normal |
| **ESR** | 18 mm/hr (0‑20) | Normal |
| **CRP** | 4 mg/L (0‑5) | Normal |
| **Urinalysis** | 1+ protein; no blood; no leukocyte esterase; microscopic: few hyaline casts | Proteinuria |
| **Coagulation** | PT 12 s (INR 1.0); aPTT 30 s | Normal |
| **Arterial Blood Gas** | pH 7.44; PaCO₂ 38 mmHg; PaO₂ 92 mmHg; HCO₃⁻ 24 mmol/L; SaO₂ 97 % | Normal |

**Imaging:**  

- **CT Head (non‑contrast, 09:55 AM):** No acute intracranial hemorrhage. Diffuse posterior cerebral edema with loss of gray‑white differentiation in occipital lobes; findings consistent with posterior reversible encephalopathy syndrome (PRES). No mass effect or midline shift.  

- **Chest X‑ray (PA, 10:05 AM):** Clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, no pulmonary edema or pleural effusion.  

**Consults:**  
- Neurology (Dr. Marko Horvat, MD) – consulted at 10:15 AM for PRES management.  
- Nephrology (Dr. Ivana Petrović, MD) – consulted at 10:20 AM for AKI on CKD.  

---

### Therapy Administered in ED  

| Time | Intervention |
|------|--------------|
| 09:15 | Two large‑bore (18 G) peripheral IV lines placed; 0.9 % NaCl 500 mL bolus started. |
| 09:18 | Oxygen via nasal cannula 2 L/min (SpO₂ ≥ 96 %). |
| 09:22 | **Antihypertensive therapy:** Nicardipine infusion initiated at 5 mg/h; titrated to 15 mg/h by 10:30 AM. |
| 09:25 | IV labetalol 20 mg bolus administered; repeat 20 mg after 10 min due to persistent MAP > 150 mmHg. |
| 09:30 | Ondansetron 4 mg IV for nausea. |
| 09:35 | Fentanyl 50 µg IV push for severe headache pain (VAS 9→4). |
| 09:40 | Levetiracetam 500 mg IV push (seizure prophylaxis in PRES). |
| 09:45 | Hold oral antihypertensives (lisinopril, metoprolol, amlodipine) pending IV control. |
| 09:46 | **Metformin 1000 mg PO BID continued.** |
| 09:50 | Continuous cardiac telemetry and non‑invasive BP monitoring every 5 min. |
| 10:00 | Repeat labs (BMP, CBC) – stable; creatinine unchanged. |
| 10:10 | CT head completed; results reviewed. |
| 10:15 | Neurology consult: recommendation to maintain MAP ≈ 110‑120 mmHg, continue nicardipine, monitor for seizures. |
| 10:20 | Nephrology consult: advise avoidance of nephrotoxic agents, monitor urine output; consider adjusting metformin if renal function deteriorates. |
| 10:30 | Nicardipine titrated to 15 mg/h; BP 180/100 mmHg; patient alert, oriented to person, time, place (GCS 14). |
| 10:35 | Plan for ICU admission; arrange transport to ICU bed. |

---

### Conclusion  

**Clinical Summary:**  
58‑year‑old male with longstanding uncontrolled hypertension, CKD‑3b, and type 2 diabetes presented with malignant hypertension (BP 242/138 mmHg) complicated by hypertensive encephalopathy manifesting as severe headache, visual disturbances, papilledema, and mild confusion. Non‑contrast CT head demonstrated posterior cerebral edema consistent with PRES. Laboratory workup revealed acute kidney injury on chronic disease (Cr 2.2 mg/dL) and a modest rise in troponin I.

**Primary Diagnosis:** Hypertensive emergency with malignant hypertension causing hypertensive encephalopathy/PRES (I16.0, G93.5).  

**Secondary Diagnoses:** CKD stage 3 (N18.3), Type 2 DM (E11.9), Essential hypertension (I10), History of MI (I25.2).  

**Disposition:** Patient transferred to the Intensive Care Unit for continued titration of nicardipine infusion, close neurologic and hemodynamic monitoring, seizure prophylaxis, and renal function surveillance. Oral antihypertensives to be re‑initiated once MAP < 120 mmHg and hemodynamically stable.  

**Recommendations:**  
1. Maintain MAP reduction ≤ 25 % within the first hour, target MAP ≈ 110‑120 mmHg, then gradual reduction to ≤ 140/90 mmHg over the next 24 h.  
2. Continue nicardipine infusion; consider transition to oral agents (e.g., amlodipine, lisinopril, carvedilol) once stable.  
3. Monitor for seizures; continue levetiracetam for at least 24 h, reassess EEG if clinical seizures occur.  
4. Daily BMP and urine output; **hold metformin** while renal function is unstable (eGFR < 45 mL/min/1.73 m²) and consider restarting only after renal function improves.  
5. Strict fluid balance; avoid nephrotoxins (NSAIDs, IV contrast).  
6. Neurology follow‑up for PRES; repeat MRI brain if neurological status worsens.  
7. Nephrology follow‑up for CKD progression.  
8. Education on medication adherence, home BP monitoring, and timely refill of antihypertensives.  

**Prepared by:** Dr. Ana Kovač, MD – Emergency Medicine Attending (02‑Feb‑2026, 10:45 AM)  

**Consultants:** Dr. Marko Horvat, MD – Neurology; Dr. Ivana Petrović, MD – Nephrology.
gold_reference: Continuing metformin in a patient with acute kidney injury on chronic kidney disease (eGFR ≈ 35 mL/min/1.73 m²) places the patient at risk for metformin‑associated lactic acidosis. Current guidelines recommend holding metformin when eGFR falls below 45 mL/min/1.73 m² or when renal function acutely deteriorates. The ED team failed to discontinue metformin, missing a critical safety intervention.

Abdominal pain in the lower part of the abdomen
**Medical History:**
- Personal History: Suffered from pancreatitis in 2010 and diverticulitis in 2016. Has had pneumonia on several occasions. Under treatment for arterial hypertension. Has known about a benign prostate tumor for the past 3 years, under urologist supervision. In March 2024, an ultrasound of the thyroid was done due to nodules, no therapy was prescribed. In May 2024, examined in the surgical department of the Emergency Department for abdominal pain; no acute surgical condition found. Recovered from COVID at the beginning of August 2024, treated with Paxlovid. Known diverticulosis of the colon and hemorrhoids. Last colonoscopy was three years ago due to alternating constipation and diarrhea. In 2022, underwent MRCP and MSCT of the abdomen (diverticuli). Examined by a pulmonologist on March 18, no elements of COPD, planned follow-up with completed tests, advised to perform LDCT as part of the NPP. Abdominal ultrasound done in October 2024. On March 20, 2025, examined in the internal department of the Emergency Department for abdominal pain and diarrhea with constipation. Abdominal X-ray: no distension, no air-fluid levels. Lab results: L 12.0, differential count: neutrophils 70.3%, Hb 157, platelets 215, prothrombin time 0.88, glucose 5.7, urea 11.6, creatinine 100, CRP 0.9, liver enzymes and bilirubin, amylase, lipase, ALP normal. Sodium 136, potassium 3.7. Advised to perform MSCT of the abdomen and then consult a gastroenterologist due to elevated CA 19-9. Has cervical and lumbar spine protrusions. On March 28, 2025, examined in the Emergency Infectious Diseases Department for fever up to 39°C with chills and shivering, abdominal pain spreading to the lower middle back, with diarrhea. CRP 69, MSCT of the abdomen and pelvis performed: appendix (aorta and branches normal, no signs of venous thrombosis, colon diverticulosis, uniform circular thickening of the sigmoid and rectal wall indicating inflammatory changes), discharged with a recommendation for Ciprinol and Medazol.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1950

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses:
    • R06.02 Shortness of breath
    • M79.1 Myalgia
    • C18.9 Malignant neoplasm of colon, unspecified
    • I10 Essential (primary) hypertension

Triage Data:
    • Triage Category: 2
    • Blood Pressure: 138/88 mmHg
    • Pulse: 108/min
    • SpO2: 91% on room air
    • Temperature: 37.1°C
    • VAS: 6 - Moderate/Severe pain
    • GCS: 15

Vital Signs:
Reason for Visit:
    • Sudden onset of shortness of breath and sharp right-sided chest pain.

Medical History:
Patient is a 74-year-old male with a history of Stage III colon adenocarcinoma, diagnosed in February 2024. Currently undergoing adjuvant chemotherapy (FOLFOX), last cycle administered 12 days ago. History of essential hypertension and Type 2 diabetes mellitus. Surgical history includes a right hemicolectomy (March 2024) and a recent arthroscopic repair of the left medial meniscus performed 10 days ago.

Current Illness:
The patient presents to the emergency department complaining of sudden-onset dyspnea that began approximately 4 hours ago while sitting. The shortness of breath is accompanied by sharp, pleuritic chest pain on the right side, which worsens with deep inspiration. He denies productive cough, fever, or chills. He notes some mild soreness in his lower extremities but attributes this to inactivity following his recent knee surgery.

Functions:
    • Appetite: Stable
    • Weight: No recent changes
    • Stool: Last bowel movement yesterday, normal consistency
    • Urination: Normal, no dysuria

Habits:
    • Former smoker (quit 10 years ago, 30 pack-year history)
    • Occasional alcohol consumption

Therapy:
    • Ramipril 5 mg 1x1
    • Metformin 850 mg 2x1
    • Aspirin 100 mg 1x1
    • Oxycodone 5 mg as needed for post-operative pain

Allergies:
    • Penicillin (hives)

Status:
    • General: Elderly male in mild respiratory distress, tachypneic, speaking in partial sentences. Oriented x3.
    • Skin: No cyanosis, no diaphoresis.
    • Neck: No JVD, trachea midline.
    • Chest: Symmetrical expansion.
    • Lungs: Clear to auscultation bilaterally. No wheezes, rales, or rhonchi.
    • Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, S1/S2 heard clearly, no murmurs.
    • Abdomen: Soft, non-tender, healing surgical scars from hemicolectomy.
    • Extremities: Left knee with arthroscopy portals healing well. Mild calf tenderness on the right, but no significant edema or erythema noted. Peripheral pulses 2+ and symmetric.

Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations:
    • EKG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 105/min. No ST-T wave abnormalities. No S1Q3T3 pattern.
    • Blood tests: WBC 6.4 x10^9/L, RBC 4.10 x10^12/L, HGB 128 g/L, HCT 0.38 L/L, Platelets 245 x10^9/L.
    • Chemistry: Urea 7.2 mmol/L, Creatinine 88 µmol/L, eGFR 74 mL/min/1.73m², Na 139 mmol/L, K 4.1 mmol/L.
    • Cardiac Biomarkers: hs-TNT 18 ng/L (Normal <14), NT-proBNP 210 pg/mL.
    • Coagulation/D-dimer: D-dimer 1450 ng/mL (Age-adjusted cutoff <740).
    • Chest X-ray (AP): No focal infiltrates, no pneumothorax, no pleural effusion. Heart size within normal limits.

Conclusion:
The patient was evaluated for sudden onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain. Given the patient’s history of malignancy, the Wells Criteria for Pulmonary Embolism (PE) was applied to determine the necessity of further imaging. The Wells score was calculated as 1.0 point (for active malignancy), which classifies the patient as low risk (low probability). Although the D-dimer is elevated at 1450 ng/mL, this is likely a non-specific finding related to the patient's recent knee surgery (10 days ago) and known metastatic malignancy. The chest X-ray was unremarkable.

The patient’s symptoms are likely musculoskeletal in nature (myalgia/pleurodynia) related to his recent surgical recovery and coughing. He was monitored in the ED; his SpO2 improved to 94% with 2L O2 via nasal cannula and he remained hemodynamically stable. 

Recommendations:
    • Discharge home with instructions to continue current medications.
    • Increase Oxycodone frequency to every 6 hours as needed for pleuritic pain.
    • Follow up with oncology as scheduled next week.
    • Return to the ED immediately if symptoms worsen or if significant leg swelling develops.

(Dr. MARKUS JURIĆ, MD, 22.08.2024 19:45)
gold_reference: The physician miscalculated the Wells score for Pulmonary Embolism. The patient actually meets several criteria: Active Malignancy (+1), Heart Rate > 100 bpm (+1.5), and Surgery within the last 4 weeks (+1.5). This totals 4.0 points, which is 'Moderate Risk' (or 'PE Likely' in the simplified version). Furthermore, if the clinician considers PE the most likely diagnosis given the sudden dyspnea and clear lungs, an additional 3 points should be added, totaling 7.0 (High Risk). By miscalculating it as 1.0 (Low Risk), the physician inappropriately dismissed the highly elevated D-dimer (1450) as non-specific and failed to order a CT Pulmonary Angiogram (CTPA).

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1995

Triage Category: 3
Diagnoses
N10 Acute tubulo-interstitial nephritis (Acute pyelonephritis)
R30.0 Dysuria
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 3; BP: 118/76 mmHg; Pulse: 108/min; SpO2: 98%; Temp: 38.9°C; VAS: 7-Severe pain; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Reason for Arrival
Right-sided flank pain and high fever.
Anamnesis
Current Illness: The patient reports a three-day history of dysuria and increased urinary frequency. Since this morning, she has developed sharp, constant pain in the right flank radiating toward the groin, accompanied by shaking chills and nausea. She measured a domestic temperature of 39.1°C before arrival. She denies vomiting, vaginal discharge, or hematuria. Last menstrual period was 10 days ago; she denies the possibility of pregnancy.
Past Illnesses: Recurrent lower urinary tract infections (UTIs), approximately twice a year. No previous hospitalizations or surgeries.
Family History: Mother has Type 2 Diabetes.
Functions: Appetite decreased due to nausea. Normal bowel movements. Frequent, painful urination.
Habits: Non-smoker, socially consumes alcohol.
Therapy: Occasional Ibuprofen for menstrual cramps.
Allergies: Denies.
Status
Of average musculoskeletal build, alert, communicative, oriented in time and space. Appears acutely ill, febrile, and moderately dehydrated. Skin is warm and dry, no rashes or icterus.
Lungs: Normal breath sounds bilaterally, no crackles or wheezing.
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm, clear tones, no murmurs. Pulse 108/min. BP 118/76 mmHg.
Abdomen: Soft, elastic. No tenderness on superficial or deep palpation of the four quadrants. No signs of peritoneal irritation. Murphy's sign negative.
Lumbar Region: Distinct tenderness to percussion over the right costovertebral angle (Lloyd's sign positive on the right). Left side non-tender.
Limbs: No edema, peripheral pulses palpable and symmetrical.
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
Laboratory Results:
WBC 16.4 [10⁹/L], RBC 4.21 [10¹²/L], HGB 128 g/L, Hct 0.385 L/L, MCV 91.4 fL, Platelets 245 [10⁹/L], NEU% 82.1 %, LYM% 12.4 %, MONO% 5.2 %, NEU # 13.46 [10⁹/L], Glucose 5.4 mmol/L, Urea 4.8 mmol/L, Creatinine 72 µmol/L, eGFR >90 mL/min/1.73m², CRP 94.2 mg/L, Na 139 mmol/L, K 3.8 mmol/L, Cl 102 mmol/L.
Urinalysis (Sediment):
Leukocytes: Large (3+), Nitrites: Positive, Protein: Trace, Erythrocytes: 5-10/hpf, Bacteria: Many.
(Dr. L. Vidić, Medical Biochemist, 18.08.2024 10:15)

Ultrasound of the Urinary Tract:
The right kidney is slightly enlarged (132 mm), with a thickened parenchymal layer and increased echogenicity, suggestive of inflammatory changes. No evidence of hydronephrosis or radiopaque calculi. The left kidney is normal in size (110 mm) and structure. The urinary bladder is partially filled, with thickened walls and sediment present. No free fluid in the Morison's pouch.
(Dr. M. Horvat, 18.08.2024 11:00)

Conclusion:
(Dr. J. MARIĆ, MD, 18.08.2024 12:30)
Based on the clinical presentation of high fever, right-sided flank pain, and positive Lloyd's sign, alongside laboratory evidence of systemic inflammation (leukocytosis, elevated CRP) and pyuria, the patient is diagnosed with acute pyelonephritis.
In the Emergency Department, the patient received 1000 mL of 0.9% NaCl IV and 1g of Ceftriaxone IV. Following rehydration and antipyretics, the patient's heart rate stabilized to 88/min and she remained hemodynamically stable. Since she is able to tolerate oral intake and has no signs of sepsis or obstruction, she is cleared for outpatient treatment.

Therapy and Recommendations:
1. Ciprofloxacin 500 mg every 12 hours for 10 days.
2. Paracetamol 500-1000 mg as needed for fever or pain (up to 4g/day).
3. Increased fluid intake (2-3 liters per day).
4. Urine culture follow-up in 48-72 hours if symptoms do not improve.
5. Mandatory follow-up with the primary care physician.
In case of persistent vomiting, inability to maintain oral hydration, or worsening of general condition, return to the emergency department immediately.
gold_reference: No critical clinical decision-making errors. Care is appropriate for the presentation.

report_content:
Date of Birth: 1995

Triage Category: 2
Diagnoses
J45.901 Acute Asthma Exacerbation
Triage Data
Triage Cat: 2; BP: 135/85 mmHg; Pulse: 118/min; SpO2: 89% (Room Air); Temp: 36.8°C; VAS: 2 - Mild chest discomfort; GCS: 15
Vital Signs
Date	RR (min)	Pulse (min)	SpO2 (%)	BP (mmHg)	Temp (°C)	GCS	AVPU
18.07.2024 09:15	32	118	89	135/85	36.8	15	A
18.07.2024 10:45	24	102	95 (4L O2)	128/80	-	15	A

Reason for Visit
Shortness of breath and wheezing
Anamnesis
Current Illness: Patient presents with acute onset dyspnea and chest tightness starting approximately 2 hours ago while cleaning the garage (dust exposure). Reports using home rescue inhaler (Ventolin) x4 puffs without significant relief. Complains of inability to speak in full sentences upon arrival. Denies fever, chills, or productive sputum.
Past Medical History: Diagnosed with bronchial asthma at age 6. Previous hospitalization in 2019. No history of intubation.
Medications: Symbicort Turbuhaler 160/4.5 mcg bid, Ventolin prn.
Allergies: Penicillin (hives).
Habits: Non-smoker, social alcohol use.
Status
Patient appears anxious, sitting in a tripod position, using accessory respiratory muscles.
Head and Neck: Trachea midline, no JVD. Mucous membranes dry.
Lungs: Diffuse, high-pitched expiratory wheezing heard throughout all lung fields. Prolonged expiratory phase. Silent chest absent (air entry audible but reduced at bases).
Heart: Tachycardic, regular rhythm. No murmurs.
Abdomen: Soft, non-tender.
Extremities: No edema, capillary refill <2 sec. Slight tremor noted in hands (likely beta-agonist effect).
Diagnostic and Consultative Examinations
12-Channel ECG: Sinus tachycardia, rate 116/min, P-pulmonale noted, no ST segment changes.
Chest X-ray (PA View):
Hyperinflation of lung fields with flattening of hemidiaphragms. No focal consolidation, pneumothorax, or pleural effusion. Cardiac size normal.
Hematology - Complete Blood Count:
WBC 11.2↗ (1e9/L); RBC 5.10 (1e12/L); Hgb 145 g/L; Hct 0.43 L/L; Plt 280 (1e9/L)
Differential: NEU% 60%; LYM% 25%; EOS% 12%↗ (Eosinophilia consistent with allergic phenotype).
Biochemistry:
Na 139 mmol/L; K 3.6 mmol/L; Creatinine 72 µmol/L; CRP 4.0 mg/L; Glucose 6.8 mmol/L.
Arterial Blood Gas (on arrival): pH 7.48↗; pCO2 30↘ mmHg; pO2 62↘ mmHg; HCO3 22 mmol/L. (Respiratory alkalosis with mild hypoxemia).
Therapy
    • Oxygen via nasal cannula 4 L/min
    • Nebulized Salbutamol 5 mg + Ipratropium Bromide 0.5 mg
    • Solu-Medrol (Methylprednisolone) 40 mg IV
    • Propranolol 40 mg orally for heart rate control
Conclusion
Patient presented with acute asthma exacerbation triggered by environmental allergens.
Following bronchodilator therapy and steroids, subjective dyspnea improved.
Wheezing persists but air entry has improved. SpO2 stable at 95% on supplemental oxygen.
Plan: Observe for 2 more hours. If stable, discharge with a short course of oral prednisone (40mg x 5 days) and instructions to step up inhaler usage.
Referral to Pulmonology for review of maintenance therapy.
gold_reference: Propranolol is a non-selective beta-blocker. In a patient with acute asthma, it blocks beta-2 receptors in the lungs, causing bronchoconstriction and potentially worsening the asthma exacerbation significantly. The patient's tachycardia is compensatory and due to beta-agonist use (Salbutamol); treating it with a beta-blocker is dangerous.
